title: Scalability
text: Scalability For coping with the more complex business applications of today and the growing number of concurrent users, adding more resources to the physical infrastructure, such as processing speed, memory, or disk space, is often done with the intention of increasing performance. However, adding hardware is not always sufficient and might not necessarily result in better performance. To efficiently use additional hardware, applications need to be scalable, that is, capable of executing pieces of the application logic on hardware specifically configured for the logic. Client/server and Web applications can typically be deployed over multiple clients and servers, ranging from a single-tier to a multi-tier architecture. The multi-tier architecture allows an application to be executed over multiple part of the infrastructure. Multi-tier architectures are typically split into the following parts: Presentation tier—Components responsible for the user interface Business logic tier—Components for business rules and task-specific behavior Data access tier—Physical data access using SQL or other DML The advantages of multi-tier software architecture include: Scalability—The deployment infrastructure is easier to partition across available hardware Deployment flexibility—Specialized component roles Enhanced infrastructure independence—The three tiers are separated Clearly defined component roles—Both the development and the reuse of components is easier Stateless communication—Between the presentation and business logic tiers using XML Component-based development—Offering the ability to segregate skill sets Was this topic helpful?
